summary:
Chogha Bonut is a Neolithic site in Khuzestan province of southwestern Iran, near the ancient site of Susa, dated to approximately 7200 to 6000 BCE. Excavated by Iranian and American archaeologists in the late twentieth century, it is recognized as one of the earliest known farming settlements in the Susiana plain — the lowland zone of southwestern Iran that would later become the center of the Elamite and Susa civilizations.

The excavations at Chogha Bonut revealed multi-phase mud-brick architecture and assemblages of stone tools, animal bones, and plant remains consistent with a mixed farming economy. The site predates the better-known Susiana Neolithic sequence associated with [[event:1475|Chogha Mish]], providing evidence that the Susiana plain was settled by farming communities considerably earlier than previously recognized. This discovery has significant implications for understanding the trajectory of social complexity in southwestern Iran, suggesting that the roots of later Elamite civilization extend back much further in time than the Bronze Age horizon that was previously most studied.

Chogha Bonut contributes to a growing body of evidence that the lowland plains of southwestern Iran — like those of Mesopotamia to the west — were drawn into the agricultural revolution from very early in the Neolithic period, and that these communities developed their own distinctive local cultural traditions while also participating in exchange networks with neighboring highland and lowland regions.
